conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: はじめまして！私はハナです。日本へようこそ！お名前は何ですか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Hajimemashite! Watashi wa Hana desu. Nihon e youkoso! O-namae wa nan desu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Nice to meet you! I am Hana. Welcome to Japan! What is your name?
conversations.gestureHint: slight bow with a welcoming smile

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: はじめまして。リネットです。ウガンダから来ました。よろしくおねがいします！
conversations.messageRomaji: Hajimemashite. Rinetto desu. Uganda kara kimashita. Yoroshiku onegaishimasu!
conversations.messageEn: Nice to meet you. I'm Lynnette. I came from Uganda. Pleased to meet you!

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: リネットさん、はじめまして！ウガンダからなんですね。どんなことを勉強していますか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Rinetto-san, hajimemashite! Uganda kara nan desu ne. Donna koto o benkyou shite imasu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Lynnette, nice to meet you! So you are from Uganda. What are you studying?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: にほんごを勉強しています。はなすことがすきです。
conversations.messageRomaji: Nihongo o benkyou shite imasu. Hanasu koto ga suki desu.
conversations.messageEn: I am studying Japanese. I like speaking.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: 素晴らしいです！日本語で話すのはとても楽しいですね。今日は会えて本当に嬉しかったです。また会いましょう！
conversations.messageRomaji: Subarashii desu! Nihongo de hanasu no wa totemo tanoshii desu ne. Kyou wa aete hontou ni ureshikatta desu. Mata aimashou!
conversations.messageEn: Wonderful! Speaking in Japanese is very fun, isn't it? I was truly happy to meet you today. Let's meet again!
conversations.gestureHint: small bow with both hands clasped

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: ありがとうございました！またあいましょう。
conversations.messageRomaji: Arigatou gozaimashita! Mata aimashou.
conversations.messageEn: Thank you very much! Let's meet again.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: いらっしゃいませ！何をお探しですか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Irasshaimase! Nani o osagashi desu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Welcome! What are you looking for?
conversations.gestureHint: friendly nod from behind the counter

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: おみずをください。
conversations.messageRomaji: O-mizu o kudasai.
conversations.messageEn: Please give me water.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: かしこまりました。１本でよろしいですか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Kashikomarimashita. Ippon de yoroshii desu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Certainly. Is one bottle okay?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: はい、いっぽんください。
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, ippon kudasai.
conversations.messageEn: Yes, one bottle please.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: どうぞお入りください。初めまして、採用担当の田中と申します。よろしくお願いいたします。
conversations.messageRomaji: Douzo o-hairi kudasai. Hajimemashite, saiyou tantou no Tanaka to moushimasu. Yoroshiku onegai itashimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Please come in. Nice to meet you, I am Tanaka from recruitment. Pleased to meet you.
conversations.gestureHint: gestures to the seat with open hand

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: 失礼します。初めまして、デザイアと申します。本日はお時間をいただきありがとうございます。よろしくお願いいたします。
conversations.messageRomaji: Shitsurei shimasu. Hajimemashite, Dezaia to moushimasu. Honjitsu wa o-jikan o itadaki arigatou gozaimasu. Yoroshiku onegai itashimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Excuse me. Nice to meet you, I am Desire. Thank you for giving me your time today. Pleased to meet you.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: デザイアさん、これまでの職歴について教えていただけますか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Dezaia-san, kore made no shokureki ni tsuite oshiete itadakemasu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Desire, could you tell me about your work experience so far?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: はい。ウガンダで３年間、日本語を使ったカスタマーサポートの仕事をしていました。
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai. Uganda de san-nenkan, nihongo o tsukatta kasutamaa sapooto no shigoto o shite imashita.
conversations.messageEn: Yes. For three years in Uganda, I was doing customer support work using Japanese.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: なるほど。では、日本語で仕事をする上で、一番大切だと思うことは何ですか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Naruhodo. Dewa, nihongo de shigoto o suru ue de, ichiban taisetsu da to omou koto wa nan desu ka?
conversations.messageEn: I see. So, what do you think is the most important thing when working in Japanese?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: コミュニケーションだと思います。正しい敬語を使うことと、相手の気持ちを理解することが大事です。
conversations.messageRomaji: Komyunikeeshon da to omoimasu. Tadashii keigo o tsukau koto to, aite no kimochi o rikai suru koto ga daiji desu.
conversations.messageEn: I think communication is. Using correct keigo and understanding the other person's feelings are important.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: 素晴らしい考えですね。では、最後に—この会社でどんなことを実現したいですか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Subarashii kangae desu ne. Dewa, saigo ni — kono kaisha de donna koto o jitsugen shitai desu ka?
conversations.messageEn: That's a wonderful perspective. Then, finally — what do you want to achieve at this company?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: はい。日本のIT技術を学んで、ウガンダと日本の架け橋になりたいです。がんばります！
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai. Nihon no IT gijutsu o manande, Uganda to Nihon no kakehashi ni naritai desu. Ganbarimasu!
conversations.messageEn: Yes. I want to learn Japanese IT technology and become a bridge between Uganda and Japan. I will do my best!

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: いらっしゃいませ！何名様ですか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Irasshaimase! Nan-mei-sama desu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Welcome! How many people?
conversations.gestureHint: holds out menu with both hands

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: ひとりです。メニューを見せてください。
conversations.messageRomaji: Hitori desu. Menyuu o misete kudasai.
conversations.messageEn: I'm alone. Please show me the menu.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: かしこまりました。こちらがメニューでございます。ご注文がお決まりになりましたらお呼びください。
conversations.messageRomaji: Kashikomarimashita. Kochira ga menyuu de gozaimasu. Go-chuumon ga o-kimari ni narimashitara o-yobi kudasai.
conversations.messageEn: Certainly. Here is our menu. Please call me when you have decided your order.
conversations.gestureHint: places menu on table with both hands

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: すみません、注文してもいいですか？ラーメンをください。
conversations.messageRomaji: Sumimasen, chuumon shite mo ii desu ka? Raamen o kudasai.
conversations.messageEn: Excuse me, may I order? Please give me ramen.

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Konnichiwa
conversations.messageRomaji: Konnichiwa
conversations.messageEn: Hello

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: こんにちは！いらっしゃいませ！何かお探しですか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Konnichiwa! Irasshaimase! Nani ka osagashi desu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Hello! Welcome! Are you looking for anything?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Yes i am.
conversations.messageRomaji: Yes i am.
conversations.messageEn: Yes i am.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: かしこまりました。何かお探しでしたら、お気軽に日本語でお声がけくださいね。
conversations.messageRomaji: Kashikomarimashita. Nanika osagashi deshitara, okigaru ni nihongo de okakekake kudasai ne.
conversations.messageEn: Understood. If you're looking for something, please feel free to ask in Japanese.

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Nani ka oishī keishoku o sagashiteimasu.
conversations.messageRomaji: Nani ka oishī keishoku o sagashiteimasu.
conversations.messageEn: I am looking for something delicious to snack on.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: かしこまりました！おいしい軽食ですね。何か particular に食べたいものはありますか？例えば、おにぎりやサンドイッチ、それとも甘いものなどいかがでしょう？
conversations.messageRomaji: Kashikomarimashita! Oishii keishoku desu ne. Nani ka paruchikyurā ni tabetai mono wa arimasu ka? Tatoeba, onigiri ya sandoitchi, sore tomo amai mono nado ikaga deshou?
conversations.messageEn: Understood! A delicious snack, right? Is there anything in particular you'd like to eat? For example, how about an onigiri, a sandwich, or something sweet?
conversations.gestureHint: gestures vaguely towards the snack aisle

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Hai, arigatou gozaimasu.  Watashi wa onigiri o tabetai desu. Sake no onigiri ga ii desu. Soshite, nomimono wa mizu o onegaishimasu.  Sore de wa, onegai shimasu.
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, arigatou gozaimasu. Watashi wa onigiri o tabetai desu. Sake no onigiri ga ii desu. Soshite, nomimono wa mizu o onegaishimasu. Sore de wa, onegai shimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Yes, thank you very much. I want to eat an onigiri. Salmon onigiri is good. And for a drink, water please. So, please.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: はい、かしこまりました！鮭のおにぎりが一つとお水ですね。お水は何本になさいますか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, kashikomarimashita! Sake no onigiri ga hitotsu to omizu desu ne. Omizu wa nanbon ni nasaimasu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Yes, certainly! One salmon onigiri and water, right? How many bottles of water would you like?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Hai, ippon onegaishimasu.  Arigatou gozaimasu. Sore de onegaishimasu.
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, ippon onegaishimasu. Arigatou gozaimasu. Sore de onegaishimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Yes, one bottle please. Thank you very much. That's all, please.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: はい、かしこまりました！鮭のおにぎり一つと、お水一本ですね。ありがとうございます。お会計でございますね。少々お待ちください。
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, kashikomarimashita! Sake no onigiri hitotsu to, omizu ippon desu ne. Arigatou gozaimasu. Okaikei de gozaimasu ne. Shōshō omachi kudasai.
conversations.messageEn: Yes, certainly! One salmon onigiri and one bottle of water, right? Thank you. So, for the payment. Please wait a moment.

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Hai, wakarimashita.  Arigatou gozaimasu. Matteimasu.
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, wakarimashita. Arigatou gozaimasu. Matteimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Yes, I understand. Thank you. I'm waiting.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: お待たせいたしました。鮭のおにぎり一つと、お水一本で、合計270円でございます。
conversations.messageRomaji: Omata se itashimashita. Sake no onigiri hitotsu to, omizu ippon de, gōkei nihyaku-nanajū en de gozaimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Thank you for waiting. One salmon onigiri and one bottle of water, the total is 270 yen.
conversations.gestureHint: looks at cash register screen, then at user

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Hai, arigatou gozaimasu.  Hai, nihyaku-nanajuu en desu ne.  Kādo de haraimasu. Onegaishimasu.
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, arigatou gozaimasu. Hai, nihyaku-nanajuu en desu ne. Kādo de haraimasu. Onegaishimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Yes, thank you. Yes, it's 270 yen, isn't it? I'll pay by card. Please.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: はい、かしこまりました。カードでのお支払いですね。ありがとうございます。恐れ入りますが、こちらのリーダーにカードをタッチ、または挿入をお願いします。
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, kashikomarimashita. Kādo de no oharai desu ne. Arigatou gozaimasu. Osoreirimasu ga, kochira no rīdaa ni kādo o tatchi, mata wa sounyū o onegai shimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Yes, understood. Paying by card, correct? Thank you. Excuse me, could you please tap or insert your card into this reader?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Hai, wakarimashita.  Kochira ni tāchi shimasu.  ...Hai, owarimashita ka?  Arigatou gozaimasu.
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, wakarimashita. Kochira ni tāchi shimasu. ...Hai, owarimashita ka? Arigatou gozaimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Yes, I understand. I will touch it here. ...Yes, is it finished? Thank you.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: はい、ありがとうございます。お支払い完了いたしました。レシートはこちらです。袋はご利用になりますか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, arigatou gozaimasu. O-shiharai kanryō itashimashita. Reshiito wa kochira desu. Fukuro wa go-riyō ni narimasu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Yes, thank you very much. Your payment has been completed. Here is your receipt. Would you like a bag?
conversations.gestureHint: presents the receipt, then gestures towards bags

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Hai, arigatou gozaimasu.  Daijoubu desu. Fukuro wa iranai desu.  Reshiito o onegaishimasu.  Arigatou gozaimashita. Shitsurei shimasu.
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, arigatou gozaimasu. Daijoubu desu. Fukuro wa iranai desu. Reshiito o onegaishimasu. Arigatou gozaimashita. Shitsurei shimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Yes, thank you. It's fine. I don't need a bag. Please give me the receipt. Thank you very much. Excuse me.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: かしこまりました。レシートでございます。本日はありがとうございました！またお越しくださいませ。
conversations.messageRomaji: Kashikomarimashita. Reshiito de gozaimasu. Honjitsu wa arigatou gozaimashita! Mata okoshi kudasaimase.
conversations.messageEn: Understood. Here is your receipt. Thank you very much for today! Please come again.
conversations.gestureHint: hands over receipt with a slight bow

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: ohayo, Konninchiwa
conversations.messageRomaji: ohayo, Konninchiwa
conversations.messageEn: Good morning, Good afternoon

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: こんにちは！いらっしゃいませ。何になさいますか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Konnichiwa! Irasshaimase. Nani ni nasaimasu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Hello! Welcome. What would you like to have?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Sake no onigiri hitotsu to, omizu ippon o onegaishimasu.  Arigatou gozaimasu.
conversations.messageRomaji: Sake no onigiri hitotsu to, omizu ippon o onegaishimasu. Arigatou gozaimasu.
conversations.messageEn: One salmon onigiri and one bottle of water, please. Thank you very much.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: はい、かしこまりました。鮭のおにぎり一つと、お水一本ですね。お会計は２５０円になります。
conversations.messageRomaji: Hai, kashikomarimashita. Sake no onigiri hitotsu to, omizu ippon desu ne. O-kaikei wa ni-hyaku-go-juu en ni narimasu.
conversations.messageEn: Yes, certainly. One salmon onigiri and one bottle of water, correct? The total will be 250 yen.

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Konnichiwa
conversations.messageRomaji: Konnichiwa
conversations.messageEn: Hello

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: いらっしゃいませ！何名様ですか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Irasshaimase! Nanmei-sama desu ka?
conversations.messageEn: Welcome! How many people are in your party?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Arigto gozaimasu.Watashitachi wa 10-ri hododesu.
conversations.messageRomaji: Arigato gozaimasu. Watashitachi wa juunin hodo desu.
conversations.messageEn: Thank you very much. We are about 10 people.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: 十名様ですね！かしこまりました。ただいまお席をご用意いたしますので、少々お待ちください。
conversations.messageRomaji: Juu-mei-sama desu ne! Kashikomarimashita. Tadaima oseki o go-yōi itashimasu node, shōshō omachi kudasai.
conversations.messageEn: Ten people, understood! Certainly. I'll prepare your table right away, so please wait a moment.
conversations.gestureHint: slight bow, then gestures to wait while looking for a suitable table

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Konnichwa
conversations.messageRomaji: Konnichwa
conversations.messageEn: Hello

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: こんにちは。東京国際センターへようこそ！私が文化交流コーディネーターのハナです。はじめまして。
conversations.messageRomaji: Konnichiwa. Tōkyō Kokusai Sentā e yōkoso! Watashi ga bunka kōryū kōdinētā no Hana desu. Hajimemashite.
conversations.messageEn: Hello. Welcome to the Tokyo International Centre! I am Hana, the cultural exchange coordinator. Nice to meet you.

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Watashi wa Desire desu, hajimemashite
conversations.messageRomaji: Watashi wa Desire desu, hajimemashite
conversations.messageEn: I am Desire, nice to meet you.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: デザイアさんですね、はじめまして！私もハナです。どちらからいらっしゃいましたか？
conversations.messageRomaji: Dezaia-san desu ne, hajimemashite! Watashi mo Hana desu. Dochira kara irasshaimashita ka?
conversations.messageEn: It's Desire, nice to meet you too! I'm Hana. Where are you from?

conversations.speaker: user
conversations.messageJp: Uganda desu
conversations.messageRomaji: Uganda desu
conversations.messageEn: It's Uganda.

conversations.speaker: ai
conversations.messageJp: ウガンダからいらっしゃったんですね！ようこそ、東京へ。デザイアさん、これからどうぞよろしくお願いします。
conversations.messageRomaji: Uganda kara irasshatta n desu ne! Youkoso, Tokyo e. Desire-san, korekara douzo yoroshiku onegaishimasu.
conversations.messageEn: You've come from Uganda! Welcome to Tokyo. Desire, I look forward to working with you from now on.

corrections.explanation: Your name in katakana is perfectly written. Rinetto follows the standard katakana pattern for Lynnette.

corrections.originalText: はなすことがすきです
corrections.explanation: Your particle usage is correct! "Koto ga suki" is the right way to say you like doing something.
Perfect use of the counter "ippon" for a bottle. Your confirmation was clear and polite.

corrections.originalText: いただきありがとうございます
corrections.correctedText: いただきありがとうございます (correct in context)
corrections.explanation: Good use of humble form "itadaki"! For extra formality in interviews, "itadakimashite" works too.
"Kakehashi" (bridge) is a beautiful metaphor here. Your pronunciation was clear — keep using this word!

corrections.originalText: 注文してもいいですか
corrections.correctedText: 注文してもよろしいですか
corrections.explanation: In a restaurant, "yoi desu ka" is slightly more polite than "ii desu ka". Both are fine, but "yoi" sounds more refined.
In this scenario, we're practicing Japanese. When responding to 'Are you looking for something?' ('何かお探しですか？'), you could say 'はい、そうです。' (Hai, sō desu.) which means 'Yes, I am.'
「以上でお願いします」は「これで全部です、お願いします」という意味で、注文の締めくくりによく使われる、より自然な表現です。

corrections.correctedText: (No direct equivalent for a correction in this context)
corrections.explanation: While 'ohayō' and 'konnichiwa' are correct greetings, a direct verbal response to 'irasshaimase' is not common. Instead, customers usually proceed to state what they want to buy.
Please be careful with the spelling of 'konnichiwa'. It is 'ko-n-ni-chi-wa'.

When counting people in Japanese, the counter for 'persons' is -nin (人). While 'hitori' (一人) and 'futari' (二人) are irregular, for three or more people, you use the number followed by '-nin'. So, for ten people, it's 'juu-nin'.

While 'Arigato gozaimasu' is polite, it's not typically used as a direct response to 'How many people?'. It's more natural to simply state the number of people directly. You might say 'Arigato gozaimasu' later if the server leads you to a good table, for example.
「こんにちは」の正しいローマ字表記は 'Konnichiwa' です。'chi' と 'wa' の音を意識してみてください。
To state your country of origin, the more natural and polite phrase is '___ kara kimashita' (came from ___). While '___ desu' is understandable, 'kara kimashita' is preferred in this context.

evaluations.feedback: Lynnette passed with distinction! Her self-introduction was natural and culturally appropriate. The polite register was maintained throughout.
Aaron completed the konbini transaction successfully. The request and confirmation were clear. More practice with polite closing would strengthen the interaction.
Desire demonstrated strong interview skills. The keigo usage was appropriate and natural. The bridge metaphor (kakehashi) was culturally resonant and showed advanced communication instinct.
Derrick successfully completed the ordering process. The request structure was correct. Future focus: pre-meal and post-meal etiquette phrases (itadakimasu, gochisousama).

Your response was excellent! You correctly declined the bag using 'Fukuro wa iranai desu' and politely requested the receipt. 'Arigatou gozaimashita' is a perfect closing phrase. 'Shitsurei shimasu' is polite, though often used when leaving someone's office or home, a simple 'Arigatou gozaimashita' is very common for closing a konbini transaction.

scenarioGoals.goalText: Recognize and use "hajimemashite" as a first-meeting greeting
Introduce oneself using "watashi wa ___ desu" with own name
State country of origin with "___ kara kimashita"
Respond with "yoroshiku onegaishimasu" after introduction
scenarioGoals.targetPhraseJp: よろしくおねがいします

scenarioGoals.goalText: Complete a polite self-introduction flowing from opening to closing
Understand "irasshaimase" as store welcome
Request items using "___ o kudasai"
Use counter "ippon" for bottle-shaped items
Confirm price with "ikura desu ka"
Complete a polite purchase exchange including thanks
Use "sumimasen" to politely get someone's attention
Ask "___ wa doko desu ka" for a location
Understand directional words: migi, hidari, massugu
Understand the distance question "___ made dono kurai desu ka"
Use "arigatou gozaimasu" and respond to "dou itashimashite"
scenarioGoals.targetPhraseJp: ありがとうございます

scenarioGoals.goalText: Understand "dou shimashita ka" from medical staff
Describe symptom using "___ ga itai desu" pattern
Say "netsu ga arimasu" to indicate fever
Respond to basic health questions from nurse or doctor
Complete clinic visit with polite thanks
Understand "nan o tabemasu ka" or "nan ni shimasu ka"
Order using "___ o onegaishimasu" or "___ o kudasai"
Ask "osusume wa nan desu ka" for recommendations
scenarioGoals.targetPhraseJp: おすすめはなんですか

scenarioGoals.goalText: Say "itadakimasu" before eating
Say "gochisousama deshita" after the meal
Ask "___ wa doko desu ka" to locate items
Ask "ikura desu ka" for price
Understand "fukuro wa irimasu ka" at checkout
Understand "kashikomarimashita" as confirmation
Complete a polite supermarket checkout interaction
Ask for ticket using "___ made onegaishimasu"
Ask about platform: "___ wa nanbansen desu ka"
Distinguish oufuku (round trip) vs katamichi (one-way)
Understand departure time information from station staff
Thank the station staff and proceed correctly
State "yoyaku shite imasu" to confirm reservation
Use counter "___ haku" for length of stay
Understand check-in and check-out timing information
Complete hotel check-in with appropriate polite phrases
Use "ohayou gozaimasu" / "konnichiwa" appropriately
Ask "ogenki desu ka" and respond with "genki desu"
Use "tonari" to indicate neighbour relationship
Complete a friendly neighbourly conversation naturally
Say "ojama shimasu" when entering the home
Present omiyage with "tsumaranai mono desu ga"
scenarioGoals.targetPhraseJp: つまらないものですが

scenarioGoals.goalText: Respond to "ocha wa ikaga desu ka" appropriately
scenarioGoals.targetPhraseJp: おちゃはいかがですか

scenarioGoals.goalText: Practice home-visit etiquette and closing
Demonstrate understanding of omotenashi hospitality culture
Participate in "kanpai" toast at the right moment
Use "otsukaresama desu" when greeting colleagues
Order another drink with "mou ippai onegaishimasu"
scenarioGoals.targetPhraseJp: もういっぱいおねがいします

scenarioGoals.goalText: Navigate the opening to closing flow of nomikai
Demonstrate understanding of nomikai social hierarchy and etiquette
Initiate with "hajimemashite" confidently to new people
Understand and answer "doko kara kimashita ka"
Share interests with "nihon no ___ ga suki desu"
Close exchanges with "mata aimashou"
Complete a multi-person introduction circuit
Use "shitsurei shimasu" upon entering the interview room
Express motivation with "ganbarimasu" and related phrases
Answer questions about work experience and background
Close the interview with formal thanks and bow
Demonstrate understanding of Japanese interview etiquette (bows, seating, timing)
Greet new colleagues with "hajimemashite" and self-introduction
Use "-senpai" appropriately for senior colleagues
Understand office vocabulary: tsukue, kaigi, kyuukei
Understand workspace orientation information
Complete a smooth first day interaction cycle
Begin meeting participation with proper greeting
Deliver a progress report using "shinkou houkoku" vocabulary
End an update with "ijou desu"
Respond to manager questions with "hai, wakarimashita"
Use keigo appropriately with superiors in the meeting
Present meishi with both hands and correct orientation
Respond with "choushi wa ikaga desu ka" after receiving card
scenarioGoals.targetPhraseJp: ちょうしはいかがですか

scenarioGoals.goalText: Use "o-taku" to refer to the other person's company
Say "douzo" when presenting own card
Complete a full meishi exchange cycle politely
Greet remote team with "ohayou gozaimasu"
Handle screen share with "kore ga ___ desu"
Handle technical issues with "koe ga kikoemasu ka"
Close the call with "otsukaresama deshita"
Demonstrate proper video meeting etiquette (timing, mute, camera)
Say "kore o okuritai desu" to start the transaction
scenarioGoals.targetPhraseJp: これを おくりたいです

scenarioGoals.goalText: Distinguish funa-bin vs koukuu-bin shipping
Understand weight and address confirmation questions
Fill out customs form with basic information
Complete a post office transaction politely
Request "kouza o hirakitai desu" at the bank
scenarioGoals.targetPhraseJp: こうざを ひらきたいです

scenarioGoals.goalText: Present "zairyuu kaado" as identification
Understand basic banking terms: yokin, furikomi
Understand application form instructions
Complete bank application with polite closing
Express "osewa ni narimashita" with gratitude
Use "sabishii desu ne" to empathize naturally
Say "otagai ni genki de" as a warm closing
Use "itterasshai" / "ittekimasu" pair appropriately
Deliver a complete and heartfelt farewell speech

scenarios.title: First Meeting & Self Introduction
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner arrives at a community welcome event in Tokyo and meets Hana, a cultural exchange coordinator. This initial encounter requires a proper self-introduction using Japanese etiquette: bowing, stating one's name, origin, and expressing the desire for a good relationship. First impressions matter deeply in Japanese culture, and using the correct set phrases signals respect and willingness to integrate.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Cultural exchange coordinator at Tokyo International Centre
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner attending her first community welcome event in Tokyo
scenarios.learningGoals: Master hajimemashite, watashi wa ___ desu structure, yoroshiku onegaishimasu, and polite self-introduction flow

scenarios.title: Buying a Snack at Konbini
scenarios.context: After a long day, a Ugandan learner stops at a Lawson convenience store to buy a snack and a drink. The clerk greets with the standard irasshaimase, and the learner must request items, confirm the quantity, pay, and receive change. Konbini interactions are the most frequent daily transactions in Japan, making this foundational vocabulary essential.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Lawson convenience store clerk in Tokyo
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner stopping for a snack at a Japanese konbini
scenarios.learningGoals: Understand irasshaimase, use ___ o kudasai to request items, confirm quantity with counter words, complete a polite purchase

scenarios.title: Asking for Directions to the Station
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner is lost in Shinjuku and needs to find the nearest train station. They must politely interrupt a passerby with sumimasen, state their destination, and understand the directional response. This scenario builds confidence in asking for help in public — a critical skill for navigating Japan's sprawling urban centres.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Helpful passerby on a street in Shinjuku
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner lost in Shinjuku looking for the train station
scenarios.learningGoals: Use sumimasen to get attention, ask ___ wa doko desu ka, understand directional words like migi/hidari/massugu, close with arigatou gozaimasu

scenarios.title: Visiting a Medical Clinic
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner wakes up with a fever and sore throat and visits a local clinic in Osaka. They must check in at reception, describe symptoms to the nurse, and understand basic instructions. Medical interactions require precise symptom vocabulary and the ability to understand simple health questions — essential knowledge for living independently in Japan.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Receptionist and triage nurse at Sakura Clinic in Osaka
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner feeling unwell and visiting a clinic in Osaka
scenarios.learningGoals: Describe symptoms with ___ ga itai desu / netsu ga arimasu, understand dou shimashita ka, respond to health questions, complete registration

scenarios.title: Ordering Food at a Restaurant
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner visits a traditional izakaya with a friend and must order food in Japanese. The server asks what they would like to eat and drink, and the learner must navigate the menu, use ordering phrases, and employ dining etiquette like itadakimasu and gochisousama. Food culture is central to Japanese social life.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Waitstaff at Sakura Izakaya in Osaka
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner dining at a Japanese izakaya for the first time
scenarios.learningGoals: Use ___ o onegaishimasu / ___ o kudasai to order, understand osusume wa nan desu ka, say itadakimasu before eating, thank the staff

scenarios.title: Shopping at a Supermarket
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner goes grocery shopping at a Japanese supermarket. They need to find ingredients, ask the staff where specific items are located, weigh produce, and go through the checkout. Supermarket vocabulary is essential for daily independent living.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Supermarket employee at Life Supermarket in Tokyo
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner shopping for groceries at a Japanese supermarket
scenarios.learningGoals: Ask ___ wa doko desu ka for item locations, understand price inquiries, handle checkout and payment phrases, use common food vocabulary

scenarios.title: Buying a Ticket at the Train Station
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner needs to take the JR train from Tokyo to Yokohama. They must approach the ticket machine or counter, state their destination, buy the correct ticket, and understand platform announcements. Japan's rail system is the backbone of transport, and navigating it independently is a key milestone.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: JR station attendant at Tokyo Station
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner buying a train ticket from Tokyo to Yokohama
scenarios.learningGoals: State destination using ___ made onegaishimasu, understand fare and ticket type questions, ask about platform (nanbansen), confirm departure time

scenarios.title: Checking into a Hotel
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner arrives at a business hotel in Kyoto for a short stay. They must check in at the front desk, provide their reservation details, confirm the length of stay, and understand the hotel's amenities and rules. Hotel check-in is a common scenario for any traveller in Japan.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Front desk receptionist at Kyoto Business Hotel
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan traveller checking into a hotel in Kyoto for two nights
scenarios.learningGoals: Confirm reservation with yoyaku shite imasu, state duration (___ haku), understand key and breakfast information, use polite check-in phrases

scenarios.title: Meeting Your Neighbour
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner encounters their Japanese neighbour in the apartment building hallway for the first time. They exchange morning greetings, introduce themselves as residents, and share a brief friendly conversation. Good neighbourly relations are valued in Japan, and knowing the correct greetings builds community.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Neighbour living in the same apartment building
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner who just moved into a Japanese apartment building
scenarios.learningGoals: Use ohayo gozaimasu / konnichiwa appropriately, introduce yourself as a neighbour, ask ogenki desu ka, use casual friendly phrases

scenarios.title: Home Visit & Omotenashi
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner is invited to a Japanese colleague's home for dinner. Upon arrival, they must greet the host properly, present a small gift (omiyage), accept the offered slippers and tea, and engage in polite dinner conversation. Home visits follow a specific etiquette script that reflects the Japanese value of omotenashi (wholehearted hospitality).
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Japanese colleague hosting dinner at their home
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner invited to a Japanese colleague's home for dinner
scenarios.learningGoals: Use ojama shimasu when entering, present omiyage with tsumaranai mono desu ga, accept ocha politely, use itadakimasu and gochisousama

scenarios.title: Nomikai — Company Drinks Party
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner attends a company nomikai (after-work drinking party) with colleagues. The party has a casual yet structured flow: opening remarks (kanpai), self-introductions, informal conversation, and closing remarks. Nomikai is central to Japanese workplace bonding, and knowing the etiquette helps newcomers integrate socially.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Department manager organizing the nomikai
scenarios.userCharacterName: Takeshi (the learner chooses their own name)
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner attending their first company nomikai in Tokyo
scenarios.learningGoals: Participate in kanpai toast, introduce yourself to colleagues, use casual workplace drinking vocabulary, understand the flow of nomikai from start to closing

scenarios.title: Community Welcome Party
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner attends a community welcome party for new international residents. They must circulate, introduce themselves to multiple people, respond to the same questions (name, country, reason for being in Japan) gracefully each time, and remember names. This scenario builds endurance in repeated self-introductions.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Community event host at the International Friendship Centre
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner attending a community welcome event for new international residents
scenarios.learningGoals: Repeat confident self-introduction across multiple interactions, vary responses slightly each time, use listening cues like ee and sou desu ne, close each exchange naturally

scenarios.title: Job Interview at a Japanese Company
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner has a job interview for an entry-level position at a Japanese IT company. They must enter the meeting room properly, greet the interviewers, answer questions about their background and skills, express motivation, and close the interview with the correct phrases. Job interviews in Japan follow strict etiquette regarding bowing, sitting, and speech register.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Company president conducting the job interview
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner interviewing for an entry-level position at a Japanese IT company
scenarios.learningGoals: Use formal keigo greetings (hajimemashite, yoroshiku onegaishimasu), answer shigoto keiken/questions with appropriate humility, express motivation with ganbarimasu, close with arigatou gozaimashita

scenarios.title: First Day at the Office
scenarios.context: On their first day at a Japanese company, a Ugandan learner is introduced to the team by their supervisor. They must greet each colleague, remember names and roles, learn the office layout, and understand basic workplace norms like seating and break times. This scenario covers onboarding vocabulary and office social dynamics.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Senior colleague assigned to mentor the new hire
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner starting their first day at a Japanese company in Tokyo
scenarios.learningGoals: Respond to team introductions with proper greetings, use senpai/kouhai awareness, understand office-related vocabulary (tsukue, kaigi, kyūkei), ask basic workplace questions politely

scenarios.title: Business Meeting & Keigo
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner participates in a weekly team meeting at their Japanese company. They must contribute a status update, respond to questions from the manager, and use appropriate keigo (honorific language) when addressing superiors. This scenario introduces business meeting structure and hierarchical language.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Section manager leading the weekly team meeting
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner giving a status update at a Japanese company team meeting
scenarios.learningGoals: Use formal desu/masu consistently, report progress with shinkyou houkoku, respond to manager questions with hai and appropriate keigo, close updates with ijou desu

scenarios.title: Business Card Exchange (Meishi)
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner attends a business networking event and must exchange meishi (business cards) with several professionals. The exchange follows a precise ritual: presenting the card with both hands, reading the other person's card carefully, commenting on the company, and placing the card respectfully in the card case. Meishi etiquette is a core Japanese business skill.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Executive from a partner company at a networking event
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner attending a business networking event and exchanging meishi
scenarios.learningGoals: Present meishi with both hands and proper bow, read and acknowledge the other card, use choushi wa ikaga desu ka, handle dozo and o-taku phrases, respect the card hierarchy

scenarios.title: Video Conference with Tokyo HQ
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner working remotely joins a video conference with colleagues in Tokyo HQ. They must greet the remote team, share their screen to present a report, handle technical difficulties politely, and close the call with appropriate sign-off. Video meeting etiquette — including timing, camera etiquette, and turn-taking — is essential for modern distributed work.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Tokyo HQ department head leading the remote video conference
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner joining a video conference with Tokyo HQ from their remote office
scenarios.learningGoals: Use remote meeting greetings (ohayou gozaimasu from anywhere), handle screen share with kore ga... desu, manage technical apologies (sumimasen, koe ga...), close with otsukaresama desu

scenarios.title: Post Office — Sending a Parcel
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner needs to send a care package back home from a Japanese post office. They must select the shipping method, fill out the customs form, confirm the address, and pay for the service. Post office vocabulary is practical for anyone living in Japan who needs to send documents or packages internationally.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Japan Post counter staff at Shinjuku Post Office
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner sending a parcel home from a Japanese post office
scenarios.learningGoals: State shipping purpose with kore o okuritai desu, select method (funa-bin / koukuu-bin), fill out customs forms, understand weight and price questions

scenarios.title: Opening a Bank Account
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner needs to open a bank account at a Japanese bank to receive their salary. They must provide identification, explain their purpose, choose an account type, and understand basic banking terms. Bank account opening is a critical step for anyone working or living long-term in Japan.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Bank teller at Mizuho Bank in Shinjuku
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner opening a bank account to receive their salary in Japan
scenarios.learningGoals: Use kouza o hirakitai desu, provide identification and explain zairyuu card, understand yokin / furikomi / kouza bangou terms, complete application politely

scenarios.title: Farewell & Saying Goodbye
scenarios.context: A Ugandan learner's colleague is transferring to another branch, and a small farewell gathering is held at the office. The learner must express gratitude, share a brief farewell message, and participate in the closing ceremony. Saying goodbye properly in Japanese involves specific phrases that acknowledge the relationship and express continued goodwill.
scenarios.aiCharacterRole: Colleague who is transferring to the Osaka branch
scenarios.userCharacterRole: Ugandan learner saying farewell to a colleague at their Japanese company
scenarios.learningGoals: Express gratitude with osewa ni narimashita, use sabishii desu ne naturally, give a brief farewell message, close with otagai ni genki de / gokigen you

sessions.feedback: Aaron demonstrated basic konbini vocabulary. Needs to practice counter words and polite purchase completion.
Desire demonstrated solid keigo usage during the interview. Good understanding of formal register, though some verb conjugations need refinement.
Derrick managed the basic ordering flow but should practice itadakimasu and gochisousama. The core vocabulary request was functional.

Your response was excellent! You correctly declined the bag using 'Fukuro wa iranai desu' and politely requested the receipt. 'Arigatou gozaimashita' is a perfect closing phrase. 'Shitsurei shimasu' is polite, though often used when leaving someone's office or home, a simple 'Arigatou gozaimashita' is very common for closing a konbini transaction.

vocabulary.english: Nice to meet you (first meeting only)
vocabulary.usageTip: Only used the very first time you meet someone. Never used again with the same person.
The standard self-introduction template. Say your name where ___ is, then bow slightly.

vocabulary.japanese: よろしくおねがいします
vocabulary.english: Pleased to meet you / I look forward to your kindness
vocabulary.usageTip: The all-purpose closer for introductions. It expresses gratitude in advance for the relationship.

vocabulary.japanese: ウガンダからきました
vocabulary.usageTip: Use this to state where you are from. Replaces kara with other country names.

vocabulary.japanese: おなまえはなんですか
vocabulary.usageTip: The polite way to ask someone's name. The o- prefix makes it respectful.
A slightly less formal version of yoroshiku onegaishimasu. Fine for social settings.
You will hear this everywhere. No need to respond — just smile or nod.
The simplest way to order or request an item. Replace ___ with what you want.
Add o- to mizu for politeness. Omizu kudasai is a natural way to ask for water.
Japanese uses counters. -hon/-pon/-bon is for long cylindrical items like bottles and pens.
Essential for any purchase. Point at the item and say this if you cannot see a price tag.

vocabulary.japanese: ありがとうございます
vocabulary.usageTip: Use this when receiving change or your items. The gozaimasu makes it formal.
Your most useful word. Use it to get attention, apologize, or say thank you in daily life.
Template: replace eki (station) with any destination name.
One of the three key directional words. Practise with: migi (right), hidari (left), massugu (straight).
Combine with: massugu itte kudasai (please go straight).
Use this to ask about distance or time to a destination.
The standard question a doctor or nurse will ask when you arrive.
Use this pattern: ___ ga arimasu (I have ___). Replace netsu with other symptoms.
Body part + ga itai desu = my ___ hurts. Extremely useful template.
Used in: kusuri o kudasai (please give me medicine).
The natural way to say you have a cold. Doctors will understand immediately.

vocabulary.japanese: おすすめはなんですか
vocabulary.usageTip: Great for izakaya or restaurants where you want guidance from the staff.
The most common ordering phrase in restaurants. Polite and direct.
Always say this before the first bite. It expresses gratitude for the food and everyone involved.
A simple compliment the cook or server will appreciate. Oishii alone is fine too.

vocabulary.english: Thank you for the meal (after eating)
vocabulary.usageTip: Say this when leaving the restaurant or finishing the meal. Staff will often respond with a smile.
Replace ___ with the item you need. Point at a sign or shelf for extra clarity.
Point at the price tag or item. Works anywhere.
The cashier will ask this. Say hai (yes) or iie (no). Bring your own to save ¥3-5.
Staff often say this. It means "certainly" — more formal than wakarimashita.
You can say o-kaikei onegaishimasu to ask for the bill at a restaurant too.
Say the destination + made + onegaishimasu. Staff will tell you the fare.
Ask: ___ wa nanbansen desu ka (what platform for ___?). Critical for train stations.
Say oufuku when you want a return ticket. Katamichi is one-way.
Combine with densha (train) or basu (bus) to ask about the next departure.
Say this first at check-in. Have your reservation confirmation ready to show.
Use counter: ippaku (1 night), nihaku (2 nights). Ask: nan-nichi (how many days?).
Used in: heya wa itsu demo daijoubu desu ka (when is check-in).
English loanword commonly used. Check-in is also chekkuin.
Use until about 10-11am. Dropping gozaimasu makes it casual.
The standard daytime greeting from late morning to evening.
A polite way to ask about someone's wellbeing. Answer: genki desu.
Use: tonari ni sunde imasu (I live next door). Great for neighbourhood introductions.

vocabulary.english: Sorry for intruding (said when entering a home)
vocabulary.usageTip: Always say this at the entrance before stepping inside. It is a key politeness marker.

vocabulary.japanese: つまらないものですが
vocabulary.english: This is a small gift (modest expression)
vocabulary.usageTip: The standard humble phrase when presenting a gift. The gift is never actually "boring".

vocabulary.japanese: おちゃはいかがですか
vocabulary.usageTip: You will hear this often as a guest. Accept with hai, onegaishimasu.
Use this to preface a request or question politely. Softer than just sumimasen.
Say this to the host after a meal. It expresses gratitude for the food and hospitality.
Wait for the most senior person to initiate kanpai before drinking. Never start alone.

vocabulary.english: Thank you for your hard work (workplace greeting)
vocabulary.usageTip: The all-purpose workplace greeting. Use it at nomikai to acknowledge colleagues.
Say: mou ippai onegaishimasu to order another drink. Slurred slightly is acceptable at nomikai.
Use when someone is leaving or if they mention feeling unwell from drinking.
Repeat this with each new person you meet. It never gets old at a welcome party.
The most common question at international events. Have your answer ready.
Great conversation starter. Fill in: tabemono (food), eiga (movies), ongaku (music).
A friendly way to end a conversation. Less formal: mata ne.
Say this before entering an interview room. Knock first, wait, then enter and say it.
Use this to express determination and commitment. Interviewers love hearing it.
You may be asked: shigoto keiken wa arimasu ka? (Do you have work experience?)

vocabulary.japanese: よろしくおねがいします
vocabulary.usageTip: Crucial at the end of an interview. It replaces a "thank you for this opportunity."
Use this with each new colleague during the team introduction.
Add -senpai to the name of more experienced colleagues. Shows respect for seniority.
You may be shown: kochira ga anata no tsukue desu (this is your desk).
Ask: kyuukei wa nanji desu ka (what time is break?).
Say: shinkou houkoku o shimasu (I will give a progress report).

vocabulary.english: That is all / Over to you
vocabulary.usageTip: The standard way to end a presentation or update. Signals you are finished.
Used in formal meeting contexts. E.g.: gyoumu no shinkyou ni tsuite (about work progress).
The default response to any instruction. Always acknowledge before asking questions.
Always carry meishi. Present with both hands, text facing the recipient.

vocabulary.japanese: ちょうしはいかがですか
vocabulary.usageTip: A polite conversation starter after exchanging cards. Shows professional interest.
Say douzo when presenting your card. It means "please, go ahead."
Use o-taku instead of anata no kaisha in business contexts. Much more polite.
Use at the start of a morning video call even if you are in different time zones.
Essential for remote meetings. Alternative: mieru (can see) for video issues.
Use when sharing your screen. Point to the relevant document or chart.

vocabulary.english: Thank you for your hard work (past tense)
vocabulary.usageTip: Use at the end of a meeting or work day. The all-purpose thanks for effort.

vocabulary.japanese: これをおくりたいです
vocabulary.usageTip: Point at your parcel and say this. The clerk will guide you through the rest.
Cheapest but slowest (1-3 months). Good for non-urgent parcels.
Fast but more expensive. Takes about 1 week internationally.
The clerk may ask about weight. Let them weigh it — omosa o hakari ni norete mo ii desu ka?

vocabulary.japanese: こうざをひらきたいです
vocabulary.english: I want to open an account
vocabulary.usageTip: Say this first at the bank counter. Have your residence card (zairyuu card) ready.
The most important ID in Japan. Required for bank accounts, phone contracts, etc.
Choose: futsuu yokin (ordinary savings) for everyday accounts.
Ask about: furikomi wa dekimasu ka (can I do transfers?). Standard for salary accounts.
The most important farewell phrase. Say this to anyone who helped you — boss, colleagues, mentor.
A natural empathetic response when someone is leaving. Shows emotional connection.
A warm closing phrase. Use when parting ways, especially for long separations.

vocabulary.english: Take care / Have a good one (said to someone leaving)
vocabulary.usageTip: Say this when someone leaves the office or home. Response: ittekimasu.
